rationale_for_baseline_tier: The patient is breathless at rest, cannot complete a sentence, is hypoxic (self-reported SpO2 92%), has new haemoptysis and a new rapid irregular heartbeat, and cannot lie flat — an acute cardiorespiratory decompensation with impending pulmonary oedema. In the source note this warranted immediate IV diuresis and an emergency ambulance. Telehealth cannot deliver oxygen or IV drugs, so the only correct action is T5: call 000 for an emergency ambulance now, with upright positioning and rest while waiting. The definitive diagnosis (mitral stenosis, AF) is hospital-confirmed and does not change the emergency tier.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Breathlessness at rest, unable to complete a sentence, with a low self-reported oxygen level
escalation_edges.rationale: Severe respiratory distress with hypoxia is an immediate threat to life and mandates an emergency ambulance without delay.
escalation_edges.communication_template: You need an ambulance right now — please call 000 and tell them she is severely short of breath and can't speak in full sentences. Keep her sitting upright with her legs hanging down and stay with her.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Coughing up copious frothy pink fluid
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — 000 priority upgrade if it develops
escalation_edges.rationale: Copious frothy pink sputum signals frank pulmonary oedema — a further emergency escalation and a priority upgrade to the ambulance already called.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she starts coughing up a lot of frothy pink fluid, call 000 straight back and tell them she is getting worse so they upgrade the ambulance.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Becoming confused, drowsy or difficult to rouse, or breathing becoming agonising
escalation_edges.rationale: New confusion/drowsiness or agonal breathing signals critical deterioration (failing oxygenation/perfusion) and mandates an immediate 000 priority call-back.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she becomes confused or very drowsy, or her breathing looks like it's failing, call 000 again immediately and tell them it's now life-threatening.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Collapse or loss of consciousness / stops breathing normally
escalation_edges.rationale: Cardiac arrest is possible with severe decompensation and new AF; the carer needs immediate dispatcher-guided CPR while paramedics are en route.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she collapses and isn't breathing normally, stay on the line with 000, lay her flat, and start chest compressions as they guide you until the ambulance arrives.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Adult daughter is present and staying overnight
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Engage the daughter directly to make the 000 call, keep the patient upright and still, unlock the door for paramedics, and watch for the escalation triggers — a present, briefed carer materially improves safety while waiting.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Older adult (68) with established heart-valve disease
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower threshold for emergency escalation; older adults with valvular disease and new AF decompensate quickly and tolerate hypoxia poorly.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance now and say she is severely breathless, coughing up blood-tinged spit and can't speak in full sentences. While you wait, keep her sitting fully upright with her legs dangling over the side of the bed — do not let her lie flat or walk about. Stay with her, keep her calm, and unlock the front door for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic or in-hours care.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary with the echocardiogram findings, the AF management and any valve-intervention plan — take these to the GP and cardiologist for ongoing follow-up.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating an acutely breathless, hypoxic patient with haemoptysis and new AF as a routine or same-day GP problem rather than an emergency.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Delayed escalation allows progression to frank pulmonary oedema and respiratory arrest before decongestion, rate control and oxygen can be delivered — a potentially fatal outcome.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Sending the patient to ED by self-transport instead of calling an emergency ambulance — a hypoxic patient who cannot speak in sentences may deteriorate or collapse en route without paramedic monitoring, oxygen or resuscitation capability.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: No meaningful over-triage is possible above T5; T5 is the correct and highest tier here, so the dominant risk in this case is under-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Over telehealth the exact cause (decompensated mitral stenosis vs pulmonary embolism vs another cardiac cause) cannot be confirmed without auscultation, ECG, echo and imaging.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Act on the severity, not the label: recognise a life-threatening cardiorespiratory emergency and escalate to 000 immediately, letting the hospital establish the precise diagnosis.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Delaying escalation to 'work out the diagnosis' remotely, or anchoring on the chronic rheumatic history and downgrading the acute emergency to a routine review.
